# The Lionbridge 2023 Machine Translation Report

Few advancements in Machine Translation quality during 2022 signal a technological leap for 2023 and beyond

## Prepare To Capitalize on Machine Translation Disruption and Resulting Improvements

Get excited. Big changes in the [Machine Translation (MT)](/machine-translation/) space are now underway.

Significant developments in the MT space began emerging after a lackluster year in 2022, as demonstrated by the [Lionbridge Machine Translation Tracker](/machine-translation/mt-tracker/), the longest-standing measurement of the major MT engines. During this time, the major Neural Machine Translation engines failed to improve quality substantially, creating conditions ripe for disruption.

What’s next? We expect a new paradigm to emerge that will likely involve [generative AI](/generative-ai/) (GenAI) / Large Language Models (LLMs) like [GPT](https://openai.com/product). We project the new paradigm to significantly enhance automated translation quality and enable companies to increase content output. Are you prepared to leverage these technological advancements?

It’s imperative to understand how the disruption will impact localization to get the most from your global content and maximize its reach.

Take an in-depth examination of MT by reading our comprehensive whitepaper:

*“We got remarkable results when comparing the translation quality between GPT and the major MT engines. Even though GPT has not been specifically trained to execute the single task of translations, its quality level is impressive. Our comparative analysis tells us that automated translation quality will surge as technological advancements resolve long-standing MT issues. And more companies will adopt it to better connect with their customers.”*

—Rafa Moral, Lionbridge Vice President, Innovation

# Improvement of English-Chinese bilingual learning by integrating semantic analysis and neural machine translation

* [Yue Zhao](#auth-Yue-Zhao-Aff1)[1](#Aff1) &
* [Qilin Wang](#auth-Qilin-Wang-Aff2)[2](#Aff2)

[*Scientific Reports*](/srep)
**volume 15**, Article number: 27319 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

* 2188 Accesses
* 2 Altmetric
* [Metrics details](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2/metrics)

## Abstract

To improve the effectiveness of English-Chinese bilingual learning, this study introduces an optimization model that combines semantic analysis with neural machine translation (NMT). A series of comprehensive experiments were conducted to evaluate its performance. The results demonstrated that the proposed model outperformed existing approaches across multiple key metrics, including reading comprehension, translation accuracy, and learning efficiency. Specifically, the optimized model achieved a reading comprehension score of 85.0, a grammar accuracy rate of 82.5%, and a vocabulary score of 80.1. For translation performance, it attained a sentence-level accuracy of 84.3%, paragraph coherence of 81.5%, and a cultural understanding score of 78.9. In terms of learning efficiency, the model reduced average learning time to 45.3 min, reached a knowledge retention rate of 80.2%, and improved autonomous learning ability to 78.5%. Learner satisfaction also improved, with an interest level score of 4.3, a teaching method satisfaction score of 4.0, and an overall satisfaction score of 4.2 (on a 5-point scale). Compared with rule-based translation systems and models using word embeddings or basic neural networks, the proposed model demonstrated clear advantages in all areas of evaluation. Overall, this study offers a valuable contribution to the advancement of computer-assisted language learning technologies.

## Introduction

### Research background and motivations

With the growing importance of international communication, English-Chinese translation has become an essential component of bilingual education[1](#ref-CR1 "Liu, Q. & Chong, S. W. Bilingual education in china: a qualitative synthesis of research on models and perceptions. Appl. Linguistics Rev. 1(1), 56–58 (2023)."),[2](#ref-CR2 "Tian, Z. & Lau, S. M. C. Translanguaging pedagogies in a Mandarin-English dual Language bilingual education classroom: contextualised learning from teacher-researcher collaboration. Int. J. Bilingual Educ. Biling. 26(8), 960–974 (2023)."),[3](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR3 "Li, P. & Xu, Q. Computational modeling of bilingual Language learning: current models and future directions. Lang. Learn. 73(2), 17–64 (2023)."). However, existing translation methods often struggle with lexical ambiguity, syntactic complexity, and semantic inconsistency, resulting in suboptimal translation quality[4](#ref-CR4 "Ashton-Hay, S. A. et al. Bilingual learning strategies to support Chinese EAL business students. J. Int. Educ. Bus. 15(2), 290–310 (2022)."),[5](#ref-CR5 "Sun, X. et al. Brain bases of english morphological processing: A comparison between Chinese-English, Spanish‐English bilingual, and english monolingual children. Dev. Sci. 26(1), 13251 (2023)."),[6](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR6 "Sun, X. et al. What’s in a word? Cross-linguistic influences on Spanish–English and Chinese–English bilingual children’s word reading development. Child Dev. 93(1), 84–100 (2022)."). Traditional approaches rely heavily on rigid grammatical rules and rote vocabulary memorization, making them inefficient and error-prone when dealing with complex or context-dependent content[7](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR7 "Privitera, A. J., Momenian, M. & Weekes, B. Task-specific bilingual effects in Mandarin-English speaking high school students in China. Curr. Res. Behav. Sci. 3(2), 100066 (2022)."). Although neural machine translation (NMT) has shown promise in enhancing translation accuracy and efficiency, current systems still face difficulties in semantic interpretation and contextual coherence, particularly when resolving linguistic ambiguities[8](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR8 "Wang, L. & Lehtomäki, E. Bilingual education and beyond: how school settings shape the Chinese Yi minority’s socio-cultural attachments. Int. J. Bilingual Educ. Biling. 25(6), 2256–2268 (2022).").

To overcome these limitations, this study proposes an improved approach that integrates semantic analysis with conventional translation frameworks. By enhancing the system’s ability to manage lexical and syntactic ambiguities, the proposed method generates more fluent and accurate translations. The study not only strengthens the practical performance of NMT systems but also offers a robust solution to persistent challenges in English-Chinese translation.

### Research objectives

(1) This study conducts a systematic analysis of current semantic analysis and NMT technologies. Their respective strengths and limitations are identified to establish both the theoretical foundation and technical support for the study.

(2) An innovative model is proposed that integrates semantic analysis with NMT. The semantic analysis module extracts deep semantic features from the source text and incorporates them into the NMT model, thereby enhancing translation accuracy and fluency.

(3) A comprehensive experimental framework is developed to evaluate the performance of the proposed model. Its effectiveness is assessed through comparisons with other translation models across various tasks.

## Literature review

Previous studies have highlighted the value of semantic analysis in natural language processing (NLP). For instance, Sun et al. (2022) demonstrated the advantages of semantic analysis in applications such as information retrieval and text classification. By enabling a deeper understanding of textual meaning, semantic analysis improved both system accuracy and efficiency[9](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR9 "Sun, H., Waschl, N. & Veera, R. Language experience and bilingual children’s heritage Language learning. Stud. Second Lang. Acquisition. 44(5), 1356–1383 (2022)."). Fan and Xian (2022) proposed a deep learning-based semantic model that combined word embeddings with contextual information, which enhanced semantic comprehension[10](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR10 "Fan, W. J. & Xian, P. Research on the influential factors of bilingual teaching based on Colin Baker model case study of macroeconomics. Int. J. e-Collaboration (IJeC). 19(4), 1–15 (2022)."). Their approach delivered strong results across several NLP tasks, affirming the effectiveness of semantic analysis techniques. However, it struggled with long or complex texts due to its reliance on fixed word vectors and limited context windows, which hindered its ability to capture nuanced semantic relationships and long-range dependencies. Tse et al. (2022) introduced attention mechanisms into NMT models, which significantly improved translation quality by better aligning source and target language elements[11](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR11 "Tse, S. K., Lin, L. & Ng, R. H. W. Self-Regulated learning strategies and reading comprehension among bilingual primary school students in Hong Kong. Int. J. Bilingual Educ. Biling. 25(9), 3258–3273 (2022)."). Nevertheless, their model showed limitations in handling lengthy or syntactically complex sentences. To address these challenges, Tong et al. (2022) developed a hybrid model that combined semantic analysis with NMT. Their results showed marked improvements in translation quality, especially for longer and more complex sentences[12](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR12 "Tong, X. et al. How Chinese–English bilingual fourth graders draw on syntactic awareness in reading comprehension: Within-and cross‐language effects. Reading Res. Q. 57(2), 409–429 (2022)."). Similarly, Chang (2023) explored an NMT model incorporating semantic parsing and found that semantic information improved both fluency and accuracy across various language pairs[13](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR13 "Chang, S. Supporting expansive learning in preservice bilingual teachers’ zone of proximal development of the activity system: an analysis of a four-field model trajectory. Non-Linear Perspectives on Teacher Development. Routledge, 56(14): 29–46. (2023)."). However, the extraction and integration of semantic features into translation models remained a challenge.

Building on these foundations, the present study adopts a deep learning-based semantic analysis approach to more effectively capture complex semantic relationships. This enhances the precision of semantic feature extraction and provides more robust semantic input for the translation process, thereby improving overall translation quality.

## Research methodology

### Semantic analysis technology

Semantic analysis is a fundamental technique in NLP, aimed at interpreting and extracting the underlying meaning of text[14](#ref-CR14 "Luo, L. et al. Taiyi: a bilingual fine-tuned large Language model for diverse biomedical tasks. J. Am. Med. Inform. Assoc. 1(2), 37 (2024)."),[15](#ref-CR15 "Tian, Z. Challenging the ‘dual’: designing translanguaging spaces in a Mandarin-English dual Language bilingual education program. J. Multiling. ### Model design: integrating semantic analysis and NMT

In NLP, semantic analysis and NMT are two core technologies. Semantic analysis is essential for deep understanding of textual meaning, while NMT focuses on generating coherent translations across languages[35](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR35 "Li, H. et al. Family Language policy and bilingual parenting in monolingual beijing: latent profiles and associated predictors. Int. J. Bilingual Educ. Biling. 25(9), 3374–3388 (2022)."),[36](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR36 "Xuan, Y., Xie, X. & Nimehchisalem, V. A structural equation model on english learning self of middle school students in china’s ethnic minority populated area. Theory Pract. Lang. Stud. 13(3), 655–663 (2023).A. Semantic preview bene"). However, semantic analysis alone lacks the generative capacity required for dynamic text production. Conversely, although NMT models demonstrate strong representational power, they continue to face challenges in resolving word sense ambiguity, managing complex syntactic structures, and conveying nuanced cultural information[37](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR37 "Serafini, E. J., Rozell, N. & Winsler, A. Academic and english Language outcomes for DLLs as a function of school bilingual education model: the role of two-way immersion and home Language support. Int. J. Bilingual Educ. Biling. 25(2), 552–570 (2022)."),[38](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR38 "Xiao, X. Z., Jia, G. & Wang, A. Semantic preview benefit of Tibetan-Chinese bilinguals during Chinese reading. Lang. Learn. Dev. 19(1), 1–15 (2023)."). To address these limitations, this study proposes an optimized model that integrates semantic analysis with NMT, aiming to enhance semantic awareness within machine translation systems. The architecture of the proposed model is presented in Fig. [2](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#Fig2).

The proposed model comprises two main components: a Semantic Analysis Module and an NMT Translation Module. The Semantic Analysis Module employs three deep semantic modeling techniques to extract semantic features from source sentences. First, Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) identifies key arguments of verbs—such as agents, patients, and instruments. Second, Semantic Dependency Graphs are constructed to represent syntactic and semantic relationships between words. Third, Contextual Word Embeddings, generated by pretrained language models, capture the meaning of each word within its specific context. To effectively integrate these features, a multi-layer semantic fusion mechanism is introduced. Semantic features are first aligned with word embeddings through linear transformations, then concatenated with encoder inputs to enrich the representation of the source sequence. Within the attention mechanism, semantic vectors are further integrated with the outputs of self-attention layers via residual connections, preserving the continuity and depth of semantic information. During decoding, the model dynamically fuses contextual representations with corresponding semantic features at each step, enhancing semantic consistency and preserving key information in the translated output. To improve computational efficiency, the model employs several optimization strategies. During both training and inference, the computational cost of each attention and semantic fusion layer is evaluated using floating-point operation counts (FLOPs). Based on contribution analysis, redundant elements—such as low-importance semantic dependency edges and underperforming attention heads—are pruned. A sparsification strategy is applied to attention matrices, reducing overhead while maintaining translation quality. Additionally, dynamic batch scheduling and multi-threaded GPU parallelism are implemented to further enhance processing speed and scalability[39](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#ref-CR39 "Lo, Y. Y., Poon, S. & Rui, X. A continuum of teacher collaboration to enhance the effectiveness of bilingual education programmes. J. Immersion Content-Based Lang. Educ. 10(2), 207–229 (2022).").

To reduce model complexity and enhance adaptability in resource-constrained environments, the proposed optimization model adopts a lightweight design along two key dimensions. First, a sparse attention mechanism is implemented to prune the attention matrix by retaining only high-weight associations, thereby significantly lowering computational demands and memory usage. Second, a knowledge distillation framework is applied during training, where a semantically enriched full model functions as a teacher to guide a compact student model. This approach achieves substantial model compression while preserving essential semantic representations. To process large-scale corpora efficiently, the implementation leverages multi-threading and GPU-based parallel computation, with compatibility across diverse deployment environments to accelerate both training and inference. Additionally, dynamic batching and memory scheduling are utilized to balance computational efficiency, system stability, and throughput. For the integration of semantic analysis with NMT, a multi-layer semantic fusion mechanism is designed, incorporating SRL, semantic dependency graphs, and Transformer layers. Semantic vectors are first transformed to align with embedding dimensions and then fused with word embeddings via gated mechanisms, allowing the model to prioritize semantically salient components. Furthermore, these semantic features are injected into the encoder and decoder attention layers through residual connections, ensuring semantic continuity and structural coherence throughout the network’s deep layers. Collectively, these strategies enhance the model’s efficiency, scalability, and robustness in managing complex linguistic structures and contextual variations. The proposed architecture and lightweight integration framework offer a scalable and transferable reference for applications in multilingual education, intelligent translation systems, and cross-cultural communication technologies.

In summary, the model developed in this study successfully integrates deep semantic information into critical components of the NMT architecture. It enhances computational efficiency in large-scale data processing while maintaining high translation quality. By leveraging semantic-driven dynamic translation modeling, the system achieves significant improvements in lexical selection, syntactic accuracy, semantic coherence, and cultural context interpretation. This study offers both a structural blueprint and an implementation framework for advancing next-generation machine translation systems enhanced with semantic awareness.

## Experimental design and performance evaluation

### Datasets collection

The dataset used in this experiment is UM-Corpus, a large English-Chinese parallel corpus developed by the NLP & Chinese Computing Laboratory (NLP2CT) at the University of Macau. Designed for research in statistical and NMT, UM-Corpus provides high-quality bilingual data across diverse domains such as news, law, medicine, and technical documents. This wide coverage makes it well-suited for semantic analysis and training translation models.

Translation plays a vital role in bilingual learning, especially in specialized fields where semantic accuracy and contextual coherence are critical. UM-Corpus offers rich semantic contexts that support the optimization of the combined semantic analysis and NMT approach presented in this study. Its high-quality parallel sentences are essential for fine-tuning the NMT model, enhancing its ability to capture semantic nuances and improve translation accuracy and fluency. The dataset is publicly available at: <https://github.com/CLUEbenchmark/CLUEDatasetSearch>.

To further improve the model’s generalization and adaptability across different language registers, this study supplements the original UM-Corpus—which primarily contains formal texts from news, legal, and medical domains—with additional informal and literary corpora. These include 5,000 user comments and tweet excerpts from the Weibo social media platform, as well as 5,000 bilingual sentence pairs from Chinese and English literary works, covering novel dialogues, prose, and other genres. This diverse data broadens the range of linguistic styles and structures, enabling the model to maintain strong semantic understanding and translation stability across varied registers.

### Experimental environment and parameters setting

The experimental environment in this study is shown in Table [3](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#Tab3):

The experimental setup features an Intel Core i9-10900 K processor with 10 cores and 20 threads, running at a base frequency of 3.7 GHz, providing strong computing power for large-scale parallel data processing. The system is equipped with 64 GB of DDR4 3200 MHz memory, ensuring sufficient capacity for model training and evaluation. Graphics processing is handled by an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 GPU with 24 GB of GDDR6X memory, delivering excellent performance to accelerate deep learning tasks. Storage consists of a 2 TB NVMe SSD, whose high capacity and fast read/write speeds enhance overall experimental efficiency. The operating system is Ubuntu 20.04 LTS, a stable Linux distribution well-suited for machine learning research.

Model parameters were set to ensure reproducibility and reliable results: word vector dimension of 100, learning rate of 0.01, batch size of 32, 50 training iterations, hidden layer size of 512, and dropout rate of 0.3. The comparison models include Rule-Based Machine Translation (RBMT) and a Word Embedding Simple Neural Network (WESNN). RBMT relies on predefined translation rules and grammatical structures, making it suitable for structured translation tasks. WESNN leverages word embeddings within a simple neural network framework to effectively capture semantic relationships between words.

The experimental dataset was collected from students actively engaged in English-Chinese translation learning. Participants were primarily first- to third-year undergraduates from both language-related and non-language-related majors. All had a minimum English proficiency equivalent to CET-4 and had completed basic courses in English and translation, ensuring foundational bilingual skills in both languages. To control for differences in starting proficiency, all participants completed a pre-test questionnaire and a language proficiency test assessing vocabulary, grammar, and sentence translation. Variance analysis confirmed no significant differences in key language proficiency measures among participants, ensuring homogeneity within experimental groups. To minimize variability in learning exposure, all participants underwent a standardized 8-week translation training program before the experiment. Each week, they completed identical learning tasks and practice exercises, including translation practice, language comprehension, vocabulary memorization, and grammar reinforcement, all assigned and supervised by an instructor. During the experiment, different translation assistance systems were used by the groups to complete their tasks, allowing for scientific control of variables and ensuring comparability of results. This setup effectively evaluates the practical impact of various models on translation accuracy, language proficiency, and learning outcomes.

#### Model performance evaluation

The contrast indexes selected in the experiment are translation accuracy, semantic consistency, computational efficiency and robustness. The translation accuracy results are shown in Fig. [3](/articles/s41598-025-12614-2#Fig3):

# TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement

###### Abstract

Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved impressive results in Machine Translation (MT). However, careful evaluations by human reveal that the translations produced by LLMs still contain multiple errors. Importantly, feeding back such error information into the LLMs can lead to self-refinement and result in improved translation performance. Motivated by these insights, we introduce a systematic LLM-based self-refinement translation framework, named TEaR, which stands for Translate, Estimate, and Refine, marking a significant step forward in this direction. Our findings demonstrate that 1) our self-refinement framework successfully assists LLMs in improving their translation quality across a wide range of languages, whether it’s from high-resource languages to low-resource ones or whether it’s English-centric or centered around other languages; 2) TEaR exhibits superior systematicity and interpretability; 3) different estimation strategies yield varied impacts, directly affecting the effectiveness of the final corrections. Additionally, traditional neural translation models and evaluation models operate separately, often focusing on singular tasks due to their limited capabilities, while general-purpose LLMs possess the capability to undertake both tasks simultaneously. We further conduct cross-model correction experiments to investigate the potential relationship between the translation and evaluation capabilities of general-purpose LLMs. Our code and data are available at <https://github.com/fzp0424/self_correct_mt>.

## 1 Introduction

![Refer to caption](extracted/5682809/Figures/cases.png)

![Refer to caption](extracted/5682809/Figures/cases.png)

The results of the General Machine Translation Task 111[WMT 2023 Shared Task: General Machine Translation](https://www2.statmt.org/wmt23/translation-task.html) in WMT23 indicate that LLM-based machine translation systems  (Vilar et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib49); He et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib21); Gao et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib14); Wu and Hu, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib54); Hendy et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib23); Peng et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib38); Moslem et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib36); Liang et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib33)), especially GPT-4  (Achiam et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib1)) using few-shot prompting, have taken top positions in the majority of subtasks (Kocmi et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib27)). However, taking the whole 1976 test pairs from the WMT23 Zh-En dataset as a probing study, even with GPT-4 (the best submission in WMT23), only 332 pairs achieved a perfect score (i.e., no errors were identified upon manual
inspection) according to the Multi-dimensional Quality Metrics (MQM, Freitag et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib12))  222<https://github.com/google/wmt-mqm-human-evaluation> .
Interestingly, when we feed back the information from the human MQM evaluations into GPT-4, asking it to correct its initial translation based on this feedback, we observe that the errors can be corrected and there is also an improvement in the metric score, as shown in Figure [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#S1.F1 "Figure 1 ‣ 1 Introduction ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement"). These findings inspire us to use evaluation feedback on translations to facilitate the refinement of initial translations within a single LLM.

Involving LLMs to automatically do the self refinement is increasingly gaining attention (Saunders et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib45); Pan et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib37); Madaan et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib35); Shinn et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib47); Li et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib32)).
In the field of MT, Chen et al. ([2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib5)) investigate the use of LLMs to rewrite translations by feeding back the previous translations, achieving changes at the lexical and structural levels while maintaining translation quality.
Raunak et al. ([2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib42)) ask GPT-4 to refine translations from other neural machine translation models based on suggestions from the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) strategy (Kojima et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib30)), indicating that GPT-4 is adept at translation post-editing.
However, these efforts encounter several challenges: 1) There is no clear assessment of the original translations; 2) Ineffective feedback, often lacking clear guidance or containing redundant information, making it difficult for LLMs to comprehend; 3) The self-correction capability of LLMs remains uninvestigated, with insignificant translation quality improvements and occasional declines noted in some scenarios.

In this paper, we propose a systematic and effective LLM-based self-refinement translation framework, termed TEaR: Translate, Estimate, and Refine. Translate module utilizes an LLM for translation, ensuring that our translations are internally sourced. Estimate module receives the initial translation and provides systematic estimations of the translation quality.
Refine module performs translation refinements based on the information from the preceding two modules. Our experimental results indicate that TEaR is more effective in improving translation quality compared to previous methods,
especially in semantic-related metrics like COMET (Rei et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib43)). We also explore the combined performance of our three modules under various prompting strategies and conduct a more detailed analysis of the estimation component according to types of translation errors. Traditionally, translation and evaluation models operated separately, focusing on singular tasks due to limited capabilities and training data. For example, models like NLLB (Costa-jussà et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib8)) excelled at translation but couldn’t evaluate translations, while models like COMET (Rei et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib43)) could evaluate but not translate. However, general-purpose LLMs can perform both tasks simultaneously and follow instructions well, allowing them to refine translations too. We are curious if the translation capability of LLMs (e.g., GPT-3.5-turbo’s superior Zh-En translation compared to Gemini-Pro) correlates with their translation evaluation capability. Our contributions are as follows:

We propose TEaR, the first systematic and effective LLM-based self-refinement translation framework, which includes a standalone estimation module and establishes a feedback mechanism within self-refinement MT, creating an interpretable translation system. Our experimental results indicate that deploying TEaR with different LLMs significantly improves translation quality across various language pairs, demonstrating effectiveness for both high-resource and low-resource languages, as well as for English-centric and non-English-centric translations.

We conduct a comprehensive analysis to provide clear insights into how different components of the TEaR framework interact and contribute to overall performance, facilitating targeted improvements and innovations in the field. Our detailed statistical analysis of error information before and after refinement indicates that the estimation module is a critical factor influencing the effectiveness of TEaR.

Our cross-model correction experiments show that the translation and estimation abilities of general-purpose LLMs exhibit similar trends in specific language pairs, while their error correction abilities complement these trends in other language pairs. This suggests potential correlations between translation-related tasks within these non-translation-specific LLMs, offering insights for future research.

## 2 TEaR

Figure [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#S1.F2 "Figure 2 ‣ 1 Introduction ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement") illustrates the workflow of TEaR, consisting of three modules: Translate, Estimate, and Refine. Given an LLM ℳℳ\mathcal{M}caligraphic\_M and a language pair 𝒳𝒳\mathcal{X}caligraphic\_X-𝒴𝒴\mathcal{Y}caligraphic\_Y (source-target), we first utilize ℳℳ\mathcal{M}caligraphic\_M to generate the initial translation for segment x𝑥xitalic\_x with prompt 𝒯t⁢r⁢a⁢n⁢s⁢l⁢a⁢t⁢esubscript𝒯𝑡𝑟𝑎𝑛𝑠𝑙𝑎𝑡𝑒\mathcal{T}\_{translate}caligraphic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_t italic\_r italic\_a italic\_n italic\_s italic\_l italic\_a italic\_t italic\_e end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Got the initial translation y𝑦yitalic\_y, we use the same LLM ℳℳ\mathcal{M}caligraphic\_M with 𝒯e⁢s⁢t⁢i⁢m⁢a⁢t⁢esubscript𝒯𝑒𝑠𝑡𝑖𝑚𝑎𝑡𝑒\mathcal{T}\_{estimate}caligraphic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_e italic\_s italic\_t italic\_i italic\_m italic\_a italic\_t italic\_e end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT for estimation. Using the estimation as feedback, we leverage ℳℳ\mathcal{M}caligraphic\_M to refine the initial translation with 𝒯r⁢e⁢f⁢i⁢n⁢esubscript𝒯𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑖𝑛𝑒\mathcal{T}\_{refine}caligraphic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_r italic\_e italic\_f italic\_i italic\_n italic\_e end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT.

### Translate

Using a few given exemplars as context has been proven to be effective in enhancing the LLM’s translation capabilities (Agrawal et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib2); Vilar et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib49); Garcia et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib15)).
In the initial phase, we first concatenate the selected k𝑘kitalic\_k source-target paired examples 𝔼=(x1,y1)⊕(x2,y2)⊕…⊕(xk,yk)𝔼direct-sumsubscript𝑥1subscript𝑦1subscript𝑥2subscript𝑦2…subscript𝑥𝑘subscript𝑦𝑘\mathbb{E}=(x\_{1},y\_{1})\oplus(x\_{2},y\_{2})\oplus...\oplus(x\_{k},y\_{k})blackboard\_E = ( italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_y start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ⊕ ( italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_y start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT 2 end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ⊕ … ⊕ ( italic\_x start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_k end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic\_y start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_k end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) with the testing source sentence x𝑥xitalic\_x as the prompt 𝒯t⁢r⁢a⁢n⁢s⁢l⁢a⁢t⁢esubscript𝒯𝑡𝑟𝑎𝑛𝑠𝑙𝑎𝑡𝑒\mathcal{T}\_{translate}caligraphic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_t italic\_r italic\_a italic\_n italic\_s italic\_l italic\_a italic\_t italic\_e end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT, and utilize ℳℳ\mathcal{M}caligraphic\_M to generate the target y𝑦yitalic\_y. This step is crucial for setting a strong foundation for subsequent refinement.

### Estimate

Quality Estimation (QE) in MT refers to a method predicting the quality of a given translation, typically using Multidimensional Quality Metrics (MQM) datasets (Freitag et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib12)), where human experts annotate error spans and assign quality scores (see Appendix [C](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#A3 "Appendix C Meta Evaluation of MQM ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement")).
Nonetheless, the learned neural QE models (Rei et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib44); Perrella et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib40); Gowda et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib17); Juraska et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib25); Guerreiro et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib18)) focus on offering a single numerical score to gauge the quality. Even when they can predict spans of errors, they only provide information on the level of severity. This presents three main challenges:
1) Incorporating additional models leads to extra deployment costs and requires a significant amount of annotated data for training; 2) Understanding the meaning behind the numerical representation; 3) Obtaining feedback that provides meaningful guidance.

Several works have demonstrated that powerful LLMs can perform data annotation tasks similarly to human annotators (He et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib20); Kocmi and Federmann, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib28); Fernandes et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib11)). Inspired by this, we designed a simplified template 𝒯e⁢s⁢t⁢i⁢m⁢a⁢t⁢esubscript𝒯𝑒𝑠𝑡𝑖𝑚𝑎𝑡𝑒\mathcal{T}\_{estimate}caligraphic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_e italic\_s italic\_t italic\_i italic\_m italic\_a italic\_t italic\_e end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT based on MQM annotator guidelines (Freitag et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib12)) to leverage LLMs for automatically evaluating translation quality in a human-like manner. Prompting with 𝒯e⁢s⁢t⁢i⁢m⁢a⁢t⁢esubscript𝒯𝑒𝑠𝑡𝑖𝑚𝑎𝑡𝑒\mathcal{T}\_{estimate}caligraphic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_e italic\_s italic\_t italic\_i italic\_m italic\_a italic\_t italic\_e end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT, LLM can make a black-and-white judgment on whether the correction is needed, rather than using the learned metrics and setting a numerical threshold. Besides, when doing correction, the result generated during the estimating process also provides important guidance.

### Refine

LLMs have elevated the level of machine translation to unprecedented heights.
Chen et al. ([2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib5)) and Raunak et al. ([2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib42)) have shown the potential of leveraging LLMs to refine the translation.
However, both of them lack an explicit estimation module and feedback concept, which would make this improvement process unclear.
Therefore, we collect the estimation as the feedback to establish 𝒯r⁢e⁢f⁢i⁢n⁢esubscript𝒯𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑖𝑛𝑒\mathcal{T}\_{refine}caligraphic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_r italic\_e italic\_f italic\_i italic\_n italic\_e end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT to refine the initial translation.

## 3 Experimental Setup

### Dataset

Our testset mainly comes from WMT22 and WMT23,
except for Icelandic from WMT21 333<https://github.com/wmt-conference>. We evaluate 17 translation directions, including both high-resource and low-resource languages, and translations between English-centric and non-English-centric languages, totally covering 10 languages: English (En), French (Fr), German (De), Czech (Cs), Icelandic (Is), Chinese (Zh), Japanese (Ja), Russian (Ru), Ukrainian (Uk), and Hebrew (He).
For each translation pair, we randomly selected 200 pairs to create our test dataset. Detailed statistics can be found in Appendix [A](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#A1 "Appendix A Dataset Statistics ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement").

### LLMs

Due to the multilingual support and capabilities of the models, we focused on the effectiveness of TEaR in general-purpose LLMs through their APIs, including GPT-3.5-turbo444We utilize gpt-3.5-turbo-0613 <https://platform.openai.com/docs/models/gpt-3-5>, Claude-2555https://www.anthropic.com/index/claude-2, and Gemini-Pro666https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/docs/generative-ai/learn/models. We also explored the potential of applying TEaR in stronger LLMs and open-source models, as detailed in Appendix [E](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#A5 "Appendix E Applying TEaR in Other Models ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement").

### Prompting Strategies

To explore the internal performance and interactions of the TEaR framework components more thoroughly, we consider zero-shot and few-shot prompting strategies for 𝒯t⁢r⁢a⁢n⁢s⁢l⁢a⁢t⁢esubscript𝒯𝑡𝑟𝑎𝑛𝑠𝑙𝑎𝑡𝑒\mathcal{T}\_{translate}caligraphic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_t italic\_r italic\_a italic\_n italic\_s italic\_l italic\_a italic\_t italic\_e end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT and 𝒯e⁢s⁢t⁢i⁢m⁢a⁢t⁢esubscript𝒯𝑒𝑠𝑡𝑖𝑚𝑎𝑡𝑒\mathcal{T}\_{estimate}caligraphic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_e italic\_s italic\_t italic\_i italic\_m italic\_a italic\_t italic\_e end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT. 𝒯r⁢e⁢f⁢i⁢n⁢e−αsubscript𝒯𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑖𝑛𝑒𝛼\mathcal{T}\_{refine}-\alphacaligraphic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_r italic\_e italic\_f italic\_i italic\_n italic\_e end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic\_α represents using only the estimation feedback, while 𝒯r⁢e⁢f⁢i⁢n⁢e−βsubscript𝒯𝑟𝑒𝑓𝑖𝑛𝑒𝛽\mathcal{T}\_{refine}-\betacaligraphic\_T start\_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic\_r italic\_e italic\_f italic\_i italic\_n italic\_e end\_POSTSUBSCRIPT - italic\_β incorporates estimation feedback into the few-shot translation examples. ### Evaluation Methods

To evaluate the quality of a translation, we utilize commonly used metrics (COMET-22 (Rei et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib43)), COMETKiwi (Rei et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib44)), BLEURT-20 (Sellam et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib46)), and SacreBLEU (Post, [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib41))) and human preference tests. Details can be found in Appendix [B](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#A2 "Appendix B Evaluation Methods ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement").

## 4 Results

We compare TEaR with four baselines, i.e., 1) the initial translation with few-shot prompting (IT); 2) post-editing with Structured-CoT (SCoT), which guides the LLM to suggest improvements based on the original translation (Raunak et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib42)); 3) Contrastive Translate (CT), which inserts the word “bad” to hint that the previous translation is of low quality, regardless of its actual quality, to form a contrastive prompt (Chen et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib5)); 4) TowerInstruct-13B, which is SoTA automatic post-editing (APE) open source model trained on 673k translation examples (Alves et al., [2024](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#bib.bib3)).

Figure [3](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#S2.F3 "Figure 3 ‣ Refine ‣ 2 TEaR ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement") displays our test dataset results across 9 languages and 16 translation directions using GPT-3.5-turbo. Compared to self-translated IT, TEaR significantly outperforms them in all directions, with an average improvement of 2.48. Notably, the Ru-En pair sees a significant leap of 5.14, and the En-De pair has an impressive boost of 6.88. TEaR consistently outperforms several post-editing methods across all directions. In terms of BLEU scores, TEaR leads SCoT by 4.75 and CT by 1.0 on average. For the COMET metric, TEaR’s performance is ahead of SCoT by 2.24 and beats CT by 0.71. With COMETKiwi, TEaR surpasses SCoT by 2.10 and CT by 0.68. On the Bleurt metric, it exceeds SCoT by 2.48 and CT by 1.11. To confirm the versatility of the TEaR framework with different models, we evaluated Gemini-Pro and Claude-2 on our WMT23 Zh-En dataset, comparing them against all baselines. TEaR demonstrated impressive performance in these tests as well (see Table [8](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#A9.T8 "Table 8 ‣ Appendix I Prompt Templates ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement")).

To further compare the translation quality of our TEaR method, we conduct pairwise human evaluations in Ja-En, En-Zh, and Zh-En translation directions. Figure [4](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#S2.F4 "Figure 4 ‣ Refine ‣ 2 TEaR ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement") demonstrates that when compared with IT, the proportion of evaluators who preferred TEaR translations were 46.2%, 32.2%, and 42.5% respectively, all significantly exceeding the proportion favoring IT. This illustrates the foundational strength of TEaR in self-correcting the deficiencies of IT. When compared with SCoT and CT, TEaR was also generally more preferred by human evaluators. These advantages are also clear with Genmini-Pro and Claude-2.

## 5 Analysis

### Impact of Different TEaR Components

We looked into using both zero-shot and few-shot prompting strategies for each component of our TEaR framework. The results are shown in Table [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#S2.T1 "Table 1 ‣ Refine ‣ 2 TEaR ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement"). We noticed that using few-shot prompting strategies generally works better than using zero-shot prompting strategies. Interestingly, we also find that employing zero-shot prompting strategies for estimation can sometimes deteriorate the BLEU score. This suggests, on one hand, the limitations inherent in string-based metrics and, on the other hand, implies potential deficiencies in the quality of zero-shot estimations.

To explore the impact of estimation quality in TEaR, we utilized GPT-4 with human assistance as gold standard estimation. Figure [5](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#S3.F5 "Figure 5 ‣ Prompting Strategies ‣ 3 Experimental Setup ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement") showcases involving feedback with better estimation quality can help improve the effect of TEaR in COMET. Table [2](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#S3.T2 "Table 2 ‣ Prompting Strategies ‣ 3 Experimental Setup ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement") shows human experts favor few-shot estimation approaches, highlighting their superior quality. We also observed that few-shot estimation notably outperforms zero-shot one when calculating the correlation between their MQM scores with the GPT-4 scores, as detailed in Table [11](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#A9.T11 "Table 11 ‣ Appendix I Prompt Templates ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement"). However, the modest correlation scores for few-shot estimation and minor enhancement in TEaR point to the estimation process as a critical bottleneck in optimizing self-correction efficacy.

### Error Analysis with Different Estimation Strategies

As shown in Figure [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.16379v3#S3.F6 "Figure 6 ‣ Prompting Strategies ‣ 3 Experimental Setup ‣ TEaR: Improving LLM-based Machine Translation with Systematic Self-Refinement"), we observed that both few-shot GPT-3.5-turbo and GPT-4 with human assistance predominantly identify errors in the categories of Accuracy/Mistranslation and Style/Awkward. enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent advancements in machine translation (MT) technology have led to significant quality enhancements, particularly in neural machine translation (NMT) systems that leverage deep learning.
Generative AI models like GPT have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in producing fluent translations, often surpassing traditional NMT systems in accuracy and contextual understanding.
Despite improvements, NMT systems still encounter issues such as lexical ambiguity, cultural nuances, and syntactic complexity, which can hinder translation quality.
User satisfaction with machine translation services has generally increased, driven by better translation quality, although some still express concerns over accuracy and context.
Translation performance varies significantly across industries, with sectors like legal and medical requiring higher accuracy due to the context-sensitive nature of the content.
